California
Fair Political
Practices Cornrnission
January 9, L992
Anthony L. uil1er
office of the Secretary of State
1230 J Street
Sacramento, CA 9 5814
Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-91-547
Dear Mr. MiIIer:
You have requested advice on behalf of Secretary of State
March Fong Eu concerning the campaign and conflict of interest
;i;;i";;;; 
provisions oi tn" potiticar Reform Act (the rrassrrl '1l
OUESTIONS
1. 
Are contributions to and expenditures made by the March
Fong Eu Foundation (the I'Foundationtr) required to be disclosed
""d6. in" campaign or conflict of interest disclosure provisions
of the Act?
2. 
Are contributions to the Foundation
l-irnits imposed on elected officials?
3. 
Are there any other concerns which
respect to Foundation activities?
subject to the gift
rnight arise with
CONCLUSIONS
Dr. Eu's involvement with the March Fong Eu Foundation will
be reportable under the Act if any of the following occur:
1. She receives rrinconetr or "gifts" from the Foundati-on,
including gifts of travel. 
such incorne and gifts must be reported
on her Statement of Economlc Interests.
2. she makes use of the donations to the Foundation for
personal purposes. such gifts will be treated as gifts to her and
irust be repoitea on her Statement of Economic Interests.
L/ 
Government Code Sections SLooo-gL015. AI1 statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
commission regulations appear at 2 california code of Regulations
Section 18000; et ses. l1I references to regulations are to Titl-e
2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations'

Our Fi■e No. A-9■―■50
Page 2
3. The Foundation is her control-Ied committee. The
Foundation is her controlled cornmittee if all of the following
occur :
a. She, her agent, or any conmittee she controls,
exercises significant influence over the actions and
decisions of the Foundation or acts jointly with the
foundation in naking expenditures; and,
b. The Foundation receives contributions of $1,0o0 or
more in a calendar year; and,
c. The Foundation receives or expends funds for the
purpose of influencing or attenpting to influence the action
of the voters for or against the nomination or election of a
candidate or the qualificatj-on or passage of any measure, or
the Foundation makes contributions to candidates or their
control-Ied committees.
where the Foundation is Dr. Eu's control-Ied cornrnittee, not
only are the Foundation's expenditures made and contributions
received reportable, but the existence of the committee nay be a
violation of the carnpaign provisions of the Act because a
candidate may have only one control-Ied committee for each
campa ign .
FACTS
Secretary of State March Fong Eu has established a nonprofit
public benefit corporation for charitable purposes naned the
rrMarch Fong Eu Foundationt' (the ttFoundationrr) . The purpose of the
Foundation is to provide grants to students to further their
education. Although the Franchise Tax Board has granted the
Foundation tax exempt status, the Foundation's application to the
Internal Revenue Service was denied pending submission of specif j.c
operating procedures.
The Foundation has remained inactive since its creation in
l-985. No funds have been received or spent by the Foundation.
Hor.rever, the directors have indicated an interest in activating
the Foundation and application is expected to be rnade in the near
future for federal tax exemption.
It is expected that Dr. Eu wil-I be personally involved in the
management of the Foundatj.on and the solicitation of funds and
authorizing expenditures for charitabl,e purposes. It is possibte
that Dr. Eu may contribute personal artwork and other personal
items to the Foundation which would be used for fundraising
activities.
ANALYS I S
A candidate, s involvement vith a nonprofit entity can become
disc l-osable under many circumstances.


Our File No. A-9■
―■50
Page 3
I. 
Statement of Economic Interest
The Political 
Reform Act was enacted by the people of the
State of californj.a by initiative 
in 1974. The purpose for the
disclosure and disqual if ication provisions of the Act was to
ensure that public officials, 
r.rhether eLected or appointed, would
perform their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused
by their own financial interests or the financial interests of
persons who have supported them. (Section 81001(b).)
Thus, under the Act, every public official 
must disclose all
his or her econornic interests that could foreseeably be affected
by the exercise of the official,s 
duties. 
(Sections 81002(c),
87200-87313.) As a public official2/ 
Dr. Eu is required to
dj,scl-ose certain statutorily specified investrnents, real property
interests, sources of incorne and sources of gifts.
A. Investments
Section 82034 provides in a pertinent part:
'rlnvestment'r means any financial interest in
or security issued by a business entitv, including
but not l-irnited to common stock, preferred stock,
rights, warrants, options, debt instruments and any
partnership or other ov/nership interest owned
directly, indirectly or beneficially by the public
official, 
or other filer, 
or his or her imnediate
family, if the business entity or any parent,
subsidiary or otherwise related business entity has
an interest in real property in the jurisdiction,
or does business or plans to do busj,ness in the
jurisdiction, 
or has done business withj-n the
jurisdiction at any tine during the tvro years prior
to the time any statenent or other action is
required under this tit1e.
Section 82005 defines I'business entity', as follows:
rrBusiness entitytt means any organization or
enterprise operated for profit, including but not
lirnj-ted to a proprietorship, partnership, firm,
business trust, joint venture, syndicate,
corporation or association.
(Emphasis added。
)
)l
"Public of f icial'r is defined in Section 82048.


Our Fi■e No. A-9■―■50
Page 4
fhis definition excl,udes organizations that are not operated
for profit. 
(Strauss Advice Letter, No. A-90-251.r/) Thus, Dr.
Eu's involvement with the Foundation as it is presently
constituted cannot be an "investmentrr as defined in the Act-
B. Gifts and Income
Public officials 
must disclose the source of any gift of $5o
or more and the source of incorne of $250 or more. However, where
persons donate money to the foundation at Dr. Eu's behest, the
donations wil-l- not be gifts to her provided they are given to the
Foundation for j.ts use. (In re Neiedlv (L976) 2 FPPC Ops. 46;
Bremberq Advice Letter, No. A-89-445. ) Under these ci.rcurnstances
donations to the Foundation would not be subject to the gift linit
irnposed on elected officials by Section 89504. Thj.s
interpretation would be different if the Foundation was acting as
an agent for Dr. Eu or a mere interrnediary for personal gifts to
her or her family. (rn re Neiedl-v, supra, at 47.)
Moreover, if in the future Dr. Eu receives 'rincomet or
ttgiftsrr from the Foundation, as defined in the Act, she will have
reporting obligations with respect to the income and gifts. 
In
addition, gifts fron the Foundation, other than gifts of travel
which are reasonably rel-ated to legislative or governmental
purposes or to issues of state, national or international public
policy, will be l-irnited to $250 in a calendar year. 
(Sections
89504, 89506.) Ptease note that a gift of travel fron a nonprofit
entity may still 
be a disclosable grift under the Act. 
(Section
82OZA; ouan Advice Letter, No. A-89-182i Ridd1e Advj.ce Letter, No.
A-89 -2 00. )
Finally, if Dr. Eu transfers campaign funds to the
Foundation, the personal use provisions of the Act restrict the
Foundation frorn providing gifts or income to her or her famity.
Under the Act, a candidate's campaign funds may not be transferred
to any nonprofit entity where a substantial part of the proceeds
wiLl have a material financial effect on the candidate or her
irnmediate farnily. (Section 89515; Stathan Advice Letter, No. I-89-
690; weiss Advice Letter, No. A-91-158. ) Staff is currently
advising that a payment of $250 or more is an appropriate standard
for what constitutes a rnaterial financial effect. 
(Weiss Advice
Letter, supra; Spain Advice Letter, I-90-410.) Thus, a paynent of
$250 or more to Dr. Eu or her fanity constj-tutes a material
financial effect within the meaning of Section 89515,

ava i l-abI e

Commission.


Our File No. A-9■
―■50
Page 5
II. 
Camoaiqn Contributions
A. controlled conmittees
The Act also requires the disclosure of campaign activities
of candidates, including expenditures made and contributions
received. (Sections 84100, et seq.) Generally, a paynent
received by a candidate is a contribution unless it is clear from
surrounding circumstances that it was received for personal-
purposes unrelated to his or her candidacy or status as an
officeholder. 
(Regulation 18215.) Thus, pa)rnents to any
organization controlled by a candidate could be considered
contributions.
when sufficient contributions are received, the organization
would qualify as a committee. (Thompson Advice Letter, No- A-88-
487.) A t'committee" is defined as follows:
Itcommitteett means any person or cornbination of
persons who directly or indirectly does any of the
fo I lowing :
(a) Receives contributions totaling one
thousand dol-Lars ($1,00O) or more in a
calendar year.
(b) Makes independent expenditures
totaling one thousand dollars (S1,000) or more
in a calendar year; or
(c) Makes contributions totaling ten
thousand dollars ($10,000) or more in a
calendar year to or at the behest of
candidates or committees.
A person or combination of persons that
becomes a committee shal■ retain its status as a
co■lm■ttee until such time as that status 
■s
terminated pursuant to Section 
§ 842■4.
section 820■3.
If the Foundation does qua■ify as a colun■tteer it may be a
"contro■■ed committee‖  pursuant to Section 820■6.  Section 820■6
defines ::controlled committee'l as fo■
lows:
"Controlled committee'r neans a cornmittee which
is controlled directly or indirectly by a candidate
or state neasure proponent or which acts jointly
with a candidate, controlled comrnittee or state
neasure proponent in connection with the making of
expenditures. A candidate or state neasure


Our File No_ A-9■
―■50
Page 6
proponent controLs a committee if he, his agent or
any other conmittee he controls has a significant
influence on the actions or decisions of the
comrnittee .
Thus, Section 82016 describes two $rays in which the
Foundation may become Dr. Eu's controtled cornmittee. First, you
have indicated that Dr. Eu will be personally involved in the
nanagement of the Foundation. Therefore, it appears that she wiII
exert siqnificant influence on the actions or decisions of the
Foundation. Where candidates are voting members of a cornmittee's
Ieadership, we have said they are presumed to be controlling
candidates since they exercise significant influence on the
actions or decisions of the foundation. (Ferquson Advice Letter,
No. A-85-044. )
Second, Dr. Eu may act in concert with the Foundation in
making campaign expenditures, By coordinating the expenditures,
she could, in effect, bolster her campaign for office through the
Foundation. Under such circurnstances, the Foundation would also
be her controlled committee. (Section 82025 and Regulation la225 l
define " expenditure. r')
Please note that Section 85201, added by Proposition 73,
provides that all contributions or Ioans made to a candidate, or
to the candj,date's controlled committee shal-l- be depos j-ted in a
single carnpaign bank account. The commission has interpreted this
to mean that a candidate for elective office mav have onlv one
campaiqn bank account and one controlled committee for each
campaiqn. (Regul-ation !852L; Riddle Advice Letter, No. A-88-
io;T41-
B. Nonprofit orqanizations
Regulation 18217 (copy enclosed) provides an al-ternate
def inj.tion of rtcontrolled cornrnittee" applicable to organi-zations
which are characterized as exempt organj.zations under Section 501
of the Internal Revenue Code, provided they are not formed or
exist primarily for political purposes. (Regulation 18217(f).)
Regulation L8 2l-7 provides :
(a) A nonprofit organization, as defined in
subdivision (f), shatl be consj.dered a controlled
committee, if both. of tt€ following app1y3
(1) A candidate, his or her agent, or
any cornrnittee he or she controls, exercises
significant influence over the actions and
4/ The comnission has created a narrow exception with respect to
candidate controlled baIIot measure comrnittees which is not u.,
issue in this letter. 
(olson Advice Letter, No. A-89-363.)


Our File No. A-9■
―■50
Page 7
decj.sions of the organization, or acts jointly
with the organization in connection with the
rnaking of expenditures.
(21 The organization qualifies as a com-
mittee under Government code Section 82013(a),
and the orqanization is operated for political
purposes. For purposes of this regulation, an
organization is "operated for political
purposesrr if either of the following applies:
(A) The organization receives or
expends funds for the purpose of
influencing or attenpting to influence
the actj.on of the voters for or against
the nomination or election of a candidate
or the qualification or passage of any
measure .
(B) The organization makes
contributions to candidates or their
controlled committees.
The purpose of subdivision (a) (2) of Regulation 18217 is to
avoid classifyinq a nonpolitical nonprofit organization as a
control-Ied committee nerelv because donations are received at the
behest of candidates .
Regulation 18217 also provides that a candidate or rnultiple
candidates may set up an organization as a nonprofit corporation
without the corporation beconing an additional controlled
cornmittee of the candidate if certain criteria are met.
(b) For purposes of subdivision (a) (1), a
nonprofit organization which is tax-exemPt under
Section 5ol- of the Internal Revenue Code, and which
is not an organization described in Section 527 of
the Internal Revenue Code, is presumed not to be
significantly 
inf J-uenced by a candidate, his or her
agent, or any commi.ttee he or she controls, if the
organization conplies \^Iith a1I of the factors set
forth befow:
(1) The.candidate is not .substantially
invol-ved in the day-to-day operations of the
organization, and the organization is
controlled by a board of directors with 3 or
more menbers, two-thirds of whon are not:
(A) Candidates,'
(B) Agents, campaign staff,
employees, or persons otherwise under the
control of a candidate; or


Our Fi■e No. A-9■―■50
Page 8
(C) Brothers, sisters, Parents,
children, spouses, brottrers- in- Iaw,
sisters-in-Iaw, sons-j.n-1aw, daughters-
in-1aw, mothers- in- Ia\., or fathers-in-law
of a board menber who is a candidate.
(2) The name of the organization does
not include the name of the candidate. For
purposes of this subdivision (b) (2) the term
"name of the candidaterr means the candidate's
first 
and last name or some other unambiguous
reference to the candidate.
(c) For purposes of subdivision (a) (2), a
nonprofit organization which is tax-exempt under.
Seclion 501 of the Internal Revenue Code, and which
is not an organization described in Section 527 of
the Internal Revenue Code, is presumed not to be
operated for political 
purposes if the organization
complies with all of the factors set forth below:
(1) The organization does not nake
contributions to candidates.
(2) The name of the organization does
not include the name of the candidate- For
purposes of this subdivision (c) (2) the term
itnarne of the candidatef means the candidate's
first 
and last narne or some other unambiguous
reference to the candidate.
(3) The organization does not spend
funds in excess of the anount permitted under
Section 5o1(h) of the fnternal Revenue code to
influence or attempt to influence legislative
action.
(4) The organization does not spend
funds to influence or attempt to inf l-uence the
qualif icati.on or passage of any measure in an
amount sufficient to qualify the organization
as a comrnittee under section 82013 of the
Government code.
(5) This subdivision (c) shall not be
construed to prevent the organization from
forming a separate and independent political
committee which is not controlled by any
person described in subdivision (b) (1) (A)-(C) .
Thus, candidates may establish and control a nonprofit
organization without it becoming an additional carnpaign committee


Our File No. A-9■
―■50
Page 9
under the Act, provided it is formed consistent with Regulation
La2]-7. (Stathan Advice Letter, No. I-90-246.) Further,
candidates may donate campaign funds to the controlled nonprofit
organization provided: (1) the nonprofit organization is a bona
fide charitable, educational, civic, religious or similar tax-
exenpt organization; (2) the expenditure is reasonably related to
a political, 
legislative or goverruuental purpose; and, (3) the
expenditure will not have a rnaterial financial effect on the
candidate, his or her j-rnmediate family, or the candidate's
campaign treasurer. 
(Section 89515. )
If you have any further questions regarding this rnatter,
please feel free to contact ne at (916) 322-59oL.
S incerely ,
Scott HaIlabrin
Acting General counsel
Cal^ U)a,tclia-u)
